segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text: I'm George Takei. My family is from California and Japan. I am from Los Angeles and New York. And this is my American story.
segments.segment_summary: Narrator introduces himself using the American Stories format, identifying his roots in California, Japan, Los Angeles, and New York.

segments.speaker: INTERVIEWER
segments.text:
George, if you can believe it. We've known each other 18 years. I just did.

I just did the math. It was nearly 18 years ago that we met in a theater in New York. You were sitting behind us, and I heard your voice. Nobody was in the theater except the four of us. It seemed. They moved us in front and. And I didn't pay attention to who was sitting behind me until I heard that unmistakable voice. You know, it was a voice I had heard all my childhood growing up, because you were the only Asian on television at the time, and so we were allowed to watch you.

As I recall, it was in the heights that we met for a second night, and I looked over and the song that was being sung was.

Inutil. The song, the Spanish word you always correct me on and and which means useless. Yes. And I look over and there you are, weeping, weeping openly among strangers. And I was so curious.

Well, you know, I had just met you, and I thought, what about that song? Could possibly. I mean, it's a good song. It's not the kind that you think would cause many people to cry, but it somehow it made you cry. And I needed to know. So I'm nosy. I went over during intermission to ask you why you cried so much during that song. Do you remember what you said.
segments.segment_summary: Interviewer recounts meeting George Takei 18 years earlier in a New York theater during In the Heights, where Takei wept openly during the song 'Inutil' (useless).

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text: It reminded me of my father when I was a teenager, having a discussion with him, uh, learning about my childhood imprisonment and American internment camps. And he was telling me the story of. How much hope my brother and I were playing. And we were near the barbed wire fence. And he said he saw that picture, the barbed wire fence and the swamps of Arkansas behind it, and his two boys playing. And he thought of all the hopes and dreams that he had of us. And here we were, imprisoned, not knowing what's going to happen, how long it's going to be, and what's our future going to be? And it said he said that was what pained him the most about being in camp.
segments.segment_summary: Takei explains that the song reminded him of his father's pain watching his two sons play near barbed wire in the Arkansas internment camp, feeling useless about their uncertain future.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text: Not to know about it. I was a five year old kid, and, uh, we went to school behind those barbed wire fences. And the teacher one morning said, we're going to begin every school morning with the Pledge of Allegiance to the flag. See that flag behind us, behind her in the front of the class room, which was a black tarpaper barrack. And she said, we're going to recite our pledge of Allegiance to that flag. And so this innocent child, a class room full of Japanese American kids, put our hands on our over our heart and pledged with liberty and justice for all. The schoolhouse window was right over there. I could see the barbed wire fence, sentry tower, and pledging allegiance to to the that country that the flag represented.
segments.segment_summary: Takei recalls reciting the Pledge of Allegiance as a five-year-old in an internment camp schoolroom made of black tarpaper, with barbed wire and a sentry tower visible through the window.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
Could not be naturalized because he was born in Japan.

Yes.

Immigrant Asians could not become American citizens. There was a law against that. And so my father spoke English fluently. But he could not become an American by virtue of his Japanese birth.
segments.segment_summary: Takei explains that his father could not become a naturalized US citizen because laws at the time barred immigrant Asians from citizenship despite his fluency in English.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
I do, I became 5th April 20th, But in May. This thing happened, which is seared into my memory. I was in the bedroom with my brother that we shared a bedroom together and my father came in, woke us up and dressed us hurriedly and said, uh, play in the living room while your mother and I do some packing. I didn't know what was happening, but my father looked very stressed. And so we did as he said, but we couldn't play because there was nothing they were packing in. Some of the things in the living room were back in the bedroom being packed. And so we decided, well, we'll just look out the front window. And we were gazing out the front window and suddenly we saw two soldiers marching up our driveway. They carried rifles with shiny bayonets on them. They stamped, stomped up the front porch, and went to the front door and began pounding with their fists on the door. Henry and I were terrorized. My father came rushing out and answered the door. And, uh, uh, he said, uh, we're packing right now. I need more time. And he said, no, bring the family out. He said, five minutes. And the soldier said, all right, five minutes. And so my father turned to go back, and one soldier raised his rifle and aimed it at my father. Henry and I were frozen. Uh, and then when the other soldier agreed to five minutes and my father started going back down the hallway. The other. The soldier that raised his rifle followed him and we were. We didn't know what was happening. Henry and I just stood there. They went into my parents bedroom and my baby sister was in a crib in their bedroom, and they finished their packing. And my father came out carrying two heavy suitcases and and small boxes under his arms. He put them down.

Only what we could carry. He gave those little boxes, one to me and one to him. He said, that's your underwear, socks, t shirts. You carry them? And he picked up the suitcases and said, follow me. And we followed him out, out onto the driveway. And we stood there waiting and waiting for my mother to come out, and we stood there and seemed like forever. But when she came out, she had our baby sister in one arm and a huge, heavy looking duffel bag and the other, and tears were streaming down her cheeks. I'll never forget that morning. It's seared into my memory. After the war and I was a teenager, I wanted to understand what all that was about. Uh, the horse stalls that we had to sleep in at at Santa Anita racetrack. And then the long three days and two night train trip to the swamps of Arkansas to the farthest east. There were ten barbed wire prison camps, and we were sent to the farthest right near the Mississippi River in southeastern Arkansas. There were two camps in Arkansas.
segments.segment_summary: Takei gives a detailed account of the morning soldiers came to forcibly remove his family from their home in May 1942, including a soldier aiming a rifle at his father, the family being allowed only what they could carry, and his mother emerging in tears carrying the baby and a duffel bag.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
I understand all that. That happened when I was a child from Arkansas to later to Tule Lake in Northern California. And so as a teenager out of camp now, I sat down with my father after dinner and and asked him all these questions. I later on discovered that there were very few Japanese American fathers who talked about that experience with their children.

Trauma. And, and I don't think many of people of my parents generation understood, as my father did, about that whole experience. But he explained to me about the ideals of America. He often talked about Abraham Lincoln's Gettysburg Address, a government of the people, by the people and for the people.

He said, it's we, the people that define democracy. But people make mistakes, sometimes horrific mistakes. And so he said, we the people have to participate, that our democracy is dependent on people participation.
segments.segment_summary: Takei describes post-war conversations with his father who, unlike most Japanese American parents, openly discussed the internment and taught his sons about democratic ideals through Lincoln's Gettysburg Address, emphasizing civic participation.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
I saw what my father did, uh, because he spoke both English and Japanese in camp. Uh, he served as a kind of a bridge with the immigrant generation and the American born, uh, generation. And particularly being in camp, he felt the importance of building a community because when we were in Arkansas, they were all into themselves. Woe is me! Uh, depressed or angry or understandably. Understandably. It's a horrific situation to be in. To lose everything. To be imprisoned. And when we hadn't done anything to to deserve that kind of treatment other than to look like the enemy. We are of Japanese ancestry, but we're Americans. We're supposed supposed to be, uh, part of this country, but we were not because we were.

The way we looked. My, my mother was forced to relinquish her citizenship. The radicals wanted to, uh, increase their numbers, and they were trying to get as many people, uh, to be on their side as possible. My mother was Married to a person from Japan. My mother was educated in Japan. My grandparents sent her to Japan to get a proper Japanese education before she came back. And so they saw her as a good candidate to be on their side. She was not. And she was. She fought them, but they put enormous pressure on. And I remember my parents going off on long walks at Tule Lake and. And when they came back, my mother's eyes were bloodshot. I, I remember she probably was crying. They were talking about her renunciation. Uh, it was a, an excruciating time for my parents. And they couldn't talk in the barracks, certainly because it was paper thin and rumors were running rampant. And if they talked outside, they would hear them talking. So they went on walks away from our our block.

My mother's decision ultimately was to renounce her citizenship, to force the government to keep Tule Lake operating as protection for our family.

It was a painful, excruciating choice for my mother, but she had to make it. And we were safe. Behind the barbed wire fence behind the barbed wire fence of Toovey Lake, protecting us from those that hated us and were shooting at white people who came to camp bearing gifts and support for us. It was a horrible, horrific time.
segments.segment_summary: Takei describes his father's role as a bilingual bridge-builder in camp, and his mother's agonizing decision to renounce her US citizenship under pressure from radicals at Tule Lake in order to keep the family together and safe.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
Elaine Collins.

We owe so much to this extraordinary American. A man who passionately loved the Constitution of the United States, who fought for so many Japanese Americans under such incredible, unbelievable pressure. And he was the one that came to the rescue of my mother. He also represented Fred Korematsu in in the Supreme Court. Gordon Hirabayashi. And Min Yasui. He was a courageous attorney. Wayne Collins is someone that I owe my who I am to. He helped define my life. Had he not stepped in and my mother and our family, we would have stayed with her. My mother, uh, when she was on that boat, the first boat going to Japan, we would have would have been with her, save for Wayne Collins. I am an American because of Wayne Collins passion for the Constitution.

It was an incredible, amazing man. Had it not been for him, I would have grown up in war wartime post-war Japan, where the people of Japan were scrabbling for whatever food they could find. We were different again, and we would probably have been hated by the people of post-war Japan.
segments.segment_summary: Takei credits attorney Wayne Collins (initially mis-transcribed as 'Elaine Collins') with saving his mother from deportation to Japan, and describes Collins' representation of Fred Korematsu, Gordon Hirabayashi, and Min Yasui before the Supreme Court.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
There were so many scary things that we heard all over America. My father's older brother, uh, went to Salt Lake City. He said, the Mormons are good people and it's going to be safe there. There was another friend of my parents who went to Chicago and said, Chicago has opportunities, but it's still a bit chancy here. My parents decided we're going to go back to someplace that we know will be very careful. My father said, I'll go first and I'll scout out how safe it is, and I'll write to you regularly every day and keep you posted. And when it's when I've found some, some place to stay and a job, I'll write to you and to come down. My father went to Los Angeles on December 22nd, 1945. My mother said she did Christmas for us with a tumbleweed of Tule Lake as our Christmas tree. I have no memory of it. I remember Christmases. They were my. My parents always made Christmas very special. Except for the Christmas of 1945. Daddy was gone and he was gone for ten weeks. He wrote to my mother regularly and at last he said, come on down. By that time our block at Tule Lake was nearly empty. They closed down our mess hall because there were so few people there, and we had to eat at the mess hall in the next block. We had a dog who was a dear, beloved pet Blackie. We had to leave him. I still remember his barking as the car that picked us up drove us away. We made that long overnight train trip from, uh, to the lake to Los Angeles, where daddy was waiting for us at Union Station. We were back in Los Angeles, but it wasn't the Los Angeles that we left. It was a scary place. Our home was on Skid Row in downtown LA. Stinking smell of urine everywhere. Leaning on the wall. Some lying on the sidewalk. The hotel that we, uh, my father had found was a flophouse, but it was filled with other Japanese Americans who had come back.

He said. Getting a job was very difficult, but it was washing dishes at a Chinatown restaurant. Only other Asians would hire us. And then he said he's also helping other Japanese Americans that are coming back find jobs. There he is washing dishes.

Still a black manager. And he and he was doing it for nothing.
segments.segment_summary: Takei describes the family's difficult return to Los Angeles after the war, including his father scouting ahead in December 1945, the family's Christmas with a tumbleweed tree at Tule Lake, leaving their dog Blackie behind, reuniting at Union Station, and their life on Skid Row where his father washed dishes in Chinatown while helping other Japanese Americans find work.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
There we were, um, my, my father, uh, quit, uh, helping people find jobs. And, uh, he found a job for himself. He found a dry cleaning store in East L.A. across across from Hazard Park, very appropriately named. It was a park across the street from where we were. And it was an all Mexican American. There were other Japanese Americans who moved into a Mexican-American area, but that was the Boyle Heights area. And a lot of Japanese Americans, uh, had a community there in Boyle Heights, but it was all a Mexican American community. Soto and Lancaster and the dry cleaning shop that my father found.

Well, Spanish was everywhere. I went to school, but the kids spoke Spanish on the street and on the playground as well. So, you know, survivor Spanish. I learned how.

Exactly.

Well, uh, I had, uh, friends with nicknames like, uh, Chichi and Pelon. And, uh, there was a twin, Tommy and Ronnie. Uh, and, but they they were bilingual. They spoke Spanish with their parents and in the family and, uh, English with me. But, uh, I learned, uh, by listening and, and venturing.

Well, they didn't speak Spanish, but my mother, uh, our next door neighbor was, uh, Mrs. Gonzalez, and she and Mrs. Gonzalez became friends, and they practically lived in each other's kitchen. And they taught each other how to make, uh, their foods. And, uh, so, uh, my mother served us enchiladas one meal, and it was delicious. And then she served, uh, tacos and the crunchy, uh, uh, fried tacos. And Henry and I had a taco eating contest, and she taught Mrs. Gonzalez how to make sukiyaki. Uh, so we had, uh, a. Culinary, uh, cultural exchange with our neighbor and linguistic exchange as well. And I learned to speak Spanish. Uh, we had the Cinco de Mayo Festival and, uh, they shared the, the culture and festivals and foods.

And it's part of what makes the United States what it is a Japanese American who appreciates and, uh, connects with the, the Mexican American culture. And sometimes when we, when we have a party in our backyard, uh, we hire a mariachi band, we go to East L.A. and, and pick a mariachi band.
segments.segment_summary: Takei describes growing up in an East LA Mexican American neighborhood where his father ran a dry cleaning shop, learning Spanish on the playground, and the cultural exchange between his mother and neighbor Mrs. Gonzalez who traded recipes for sukiyaki, enchiladas, and tacos.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
Well, uh, in junior high school, when I was volunteering for the Red cross, um, there were African Americans that joined us and there were African Americans at, uh, uh, Mount Vernon Junior High School as well. Uh, one guy, Ron Giles, was a good friend of mine. And, uh, uh, in the papers, there are stories of what's happening in little Rock, Arkansas and Arkansas was where I spent a part of my childhood so.

And my father subscribed to the Los Angeles Times, and we're reading papers and doing our after dinner conversations. The civil rights movement came up and my father said, it's just like what happened to us because we look like this. We were. We went through the internment experience and in camp two, he told us about African Americans as slaves. So I knew about the slave history of the United States, and he said, we. After dinner, we talked about the headlines and all. And he said, the civil rights movement is the result of slavery. And it's like us. We were in prison because we look like this, just like the enemy. And the African people were brought to the United States as a cargo and sold as property. Uh, and so they have that history. And here this is going on right now. And, uh, and I read about, uh, Doctor Martin, Martin Luther King in the newspapers, and we talked about that and he said it's the same thing that happened to us. It's happening to black people and it's a story of America. Participate. They're participating in the American story. Doctor Martin Luther King makes those beautiful speeches. And so I got involved in the civil rights movement at school. And, uh, I auditioned for a production of, uh, of a civil rights musical called Fly Blackbird.
segments.segment_summary: Takei describes his path into civil rights activism through after-dinner conversations with his father connecting Japanese internment to Black slavery, friendships with African Americans at Mount Vernon Junior High, reading about Little Rock and Dr. King, and auditioning for the civil rights musical Fly Blackbird.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
I had a quite a resume. I was called in for an interview with this man named Gene Roddenberry.

I it was a science fiction project, and he was explaining to me the background behind this idea that he has it's place in the 23rd century off in the future in a starship soaring through the galaxies, solving, uh, the.

Galactic challenges that, uh, that beings are facing, not just humans. I had a good conversation with him. It was a lot of fun talking well about politics of the time. And, uh, and I walked out feeling kind of puzzled. I loved the conversation. I was unsure whether he liked me or not. I wanted the part. I desperately wanted a part. I mean, it was a regular role. And that's steady employment, right? And it was a character that's part of the leadership team of this futuristic society, three centuries ahead.

That was part of our conversation that, uh, by the, the 23rd century, we would have grown to appreciate our diversity. Uh, we have a turbulent background. Uh, world War Two happened and it was a, for you, a horrible experience, Gene Roddenberry said. And here you are. You are contributing to our society. And that's the kind of world that we want to project in the future. And I got cast in it. And, uh, he said I had a problem with the Asian character because, uh, every Asian surname is nationally specific. Uh, Tanaka is Japanese, Wong is Chinese, Kim is Korean. And 20th century Asia is turbulent with warfare, colonization, uh. Aspects of civilization that he doesn't want to, uh, represent. And he wanted to find a name that could be considered pan-Asian and he had a map of Asia pinned on his wall. And he said he was studying that map. And he saw off the coast of the Philippines, the Sulu Sea. And he thought the waters of a sea touch all shores. He had the name for that Asian character. And that's the character I'm considering you for. And as it turned out, I was the guy who was going to give flesh and blood to Sulu, and he wanted to show the diversity of the crew of the Starship Enterprise to represent all of humankind and alien kind as well, as it turns out, with Spock. It was that diversity that was the strength of the starship People who, who's who are able to work together in concert, each contributing our own unique talent and background knowledge and personality to making the Starship enterprise not only an efficient place, uh, problem solving place, but also an engaging, vital place. That diversity was what made the life on the Starship Enterprise, uh, a good, uh, creative, productive experience.

There was an acronym IDEC ID. I see infinite diversity in infinite combinations, and that's what the cast of the starship was to represent, and my character Sulu would represent Asia. All of the vast and diverse. History of Asia would be personified.
segments.segment_summary: Takei recounts his interview with Gene Roddenberry for Star Trek, Roddenberry's vision of 23rd-century diversity, the origin of the character name Sulu from the Sulu Sea as a pan-Asian symbol, and the philosophy of Infinite Diversity in Infinite Combinations (IDIC).

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
This is 1960s. You're too young to understand what that area was. Eero was like, uh. There was no way that a young actor can hope to have an acting career and be known as a gay actor, because no producer would hire you. I desperately wanted not only an acting career, but particularly that role. It was a wonderful opportunity, and all actors who were gay at that time were closeted. Now we know that one of the great dramatic actors of that period, Rock Hudson, was gay, but even he, with the power that, uh. Of a big romantic star brings with him, could not be out as a gay actor and hope to maintain his career. My heartthrob as a teenager was a blond, good looking guy named Tab Hunter.

And so there you are. You could not be gay and out at that time. So I had to be closeted and being an, a Japanese American who had that, uh, that internment experience. Uh, and I was trying to, uh, put that in a positive context by being visible and, uh, active as an actor. It would be working against that by ruining and acting opportunity. The. I had to be realistic and that was the the real reality of the times.
segments.segment_summary: Takei describes the impossibility of being openly gay in 1960s Hollywood, citing Rock Hudson and Tab Hunter as examples, and explains why he remained closeted to protect both his career and his ability to represent Japanese Americans positively.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
Came very close, good friends, but I felt secure in, uh, in being who I am and not being out with them. However, in little clues, they let me know that they knew. Uh. We would have a, a lot of extras in some scenes, uh, young cadets. And they would be in fitting together. And it's, it's the beginning of the day. And, and we usually congregated around the coffee earned. And Walter Koenig and I would be there sipping.

Chekhov? Yes. Coffee. And then a group of extras would come trotting in. In the costume, and they're coming in behind me, and Walter's here and I'm drinking. Walter suddenly, suddenly starts going. Oh, he wants me to look at something over there. So I turned around and looked. And there's this gorgeous hunk, tight fitting Starfleet uniform and that face. I mean, he was going to be a matinee idol before too long. And my heart stopped. And then I realized it's. Walter then urged me to look over there and I looked at Walter, and Walter goes. He was helping me.

He didn't want me to miss out on something.
segments.segment_summary: Takei shares an anecdote about castmate Walter Koenig (Chekhov) subtly signaling awareness of Takei's sexuality by nudging him to notice an attractive male extra on the Star Trek set, illustrating the unspoken acceptance among close friends.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
Well, the the world was changing. Star Trek fans generally knew because Brad came with me to the conventions. Things were starting to change. And, uh, uh. Stonewall happened. And many of the people, uh, involved in Stonewall itself, but others that took inspiration from it became activists and vocal and campaigning for LGBTQ rights and recognition. And because of their activities, they were tilling the soil for an America to to accept and embrace people who are LGBTQ. And because of their activism, things were starting to happen. Marriage equality became an issue that people talked about, acted on, and places were starting to recognize the inequality of the situation and all that was contributing to legalistic, uh, Change happening in. 2003, Massachusetts got marriage equality for LGBTQ people. It was a landmark event. And in California, the issue of LGBTQ equality in marriage became a big issue. And both houses of the California legislature, the Assembly and the Senate passed the equality bill. We were excited, and it was almost assumed that it would become law in California, except that we had a governor, Arnold Schwarzenegger. arch conservative political right. But both houses representing the people of California had passed the bill. And I thought, surely this guy's got to recognize the will of the people. But when the bill landed on his desk, he vetoed it. After all the sounds that he made. And when the press asked for his reaction to that veto and they stuck the mic in front of him, he just kept repeating the same one phrase. I have no problem with it, but I vetoed it. Well, why did you veto it? I have no problem with it, but I vetoed it. He kept repeating that asinine response over and over.

It was. I mean, not only was it shocking, it was infuriating. It made us more inferior and infuriated. And that's when I said to Brad, this is it. America is changing and we have to change and we're going to help it change. And I said, I'm coming out. And Brad was very supportive.
segments.segment_summary: Takei describes the path to his 2005 coming out, from Stonewall's legacy to Massachusetts marriage equality in 2003 to Arnold Schwarzenegger's veto of California's marriage equality bill, which became the catalyst for his decision to publicly come out with partner Brad's support.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text:
Well, I went we were supporters of the Human Rights Campaign, of which you are one of the leaders. And, uh, uh, I said, uh, I've decided to come out. You have experience in it. I'd like to work with you in making a marriage equality and equality for LGBT, LGBTQ people of reality for America. I've been active in the civil rights movement, in political electoral politics, and. And I like to bring that to this effort for our struggle for equality. And they arranged a series of speaking engagements, uh, all over America. And I spoke both on the internment of Japanese Americans and relating it to equality for LGBTQ people.

Same story, Same issue. Anyone who's different in any way is somehow inferior. And they need they need it to be legislated against. But in order to recognize the contribution that we can all make to making our democracy a truer democracy, to recognize. We are fallible human beings and we make mistakes. Even a great president. And my father admired Roosevelt during the 30s. He he said he was a great leader, but even he was a fallible human being. And he put innocent people in prison camps just because we look different. And it was a rational, crazy, stupid, cruel, painful act that he took. And his wife, Eleanor Roosevelt, knew better.

I guess you're right. I did become a catalyst of sorts, and I'm proud to have been able to make that contribution. It's what we're all doing and what Star Trek is. Infinite diversity in infinite combinations, and that infinite combinations is the strength of our our democracy.
segments.segment_summary: Takei describes working with the Human Rights Campaign after coming out, connecting Japanese internment and LGBTQ inequality as the same story of prejudice, and reflecting on FDR's fallibility and Eleanor Roosevelt's moral clarity.

segments.speaker: GEORGE TAKEI
segments.text: People say democracy is fragile, but I've come to understand the strength of our democracy. It's in us, but we are fragile. We get swept up slavery. That mentality that puts people of a different color in a category as property internment. Uh, inequality for LGBTQ. We are the ones that are fragile. We have to recognize that we are the strength of our democracy, and we have to actively participate in making that notion of our diversity being a strength. As Gene Roddenberry said, infinite diversity and infinite combinations. And when you stop to think about it, that's what makes life that much more vibrant, more exciting, more interesting. And that's what we have to convey to the future. We have to engage to make our democracy vibrant.
segments.segment_summary: Takei delivers his closing message that democracy is not fragile but people are, connecting slavery, internment, and LGBTQ inequality as examples, and invoking Roddenberry's IDIC philosophy as a call to active civic participation.
